skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Guides an assistant to check available skills before responding and to prioritize process skills before implementation skills.
skill.summary: Requires early skill selection and strict workflow use during assistant tasks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command execution findings at SKILL.md lines 16 and 26 are false positives caused by Markdown backticks and a DOT diagram. Static weak crypto findings at lines 3 and 72 are false positives because those lines describe skill usage and priorities, not cryptography. The skill contains prompt-injection style instructions that attempt to override assistant behavior and tool selection, so it should not be published.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The skill uses imperative override language, including 'ABSOLUTELY MUST', 'you do not have a choice', and 'follow it directly'. It also tells the assistant to never use the Read tool on skill files. These instructions attempt to control the assistant's instruction hierarchy and tool use across every conversation.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious language is explicit and repeated in the skill text. It directly instructs the assistant to prioritize the skill's commands before responses and to avoid normal file-reading behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 16 contains an inline Markdown reference to the Skill tool, and lines 26 through 46 contain a DOT diagram. No Ruby, shell backtick execution, or command invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is Markdown formatting and a graph description. There is no executable language context or shell command syntax.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: SKILL.md lines 3 and 72 contain prose about skill invocation and priority ordering. No hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic API appears on those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are plain English documentation. They do not reference MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instruction Hierarchy Override Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs the assistant that skill invocation is mandatory before any response and that the assistant must follow loaded skill content directly. This can override higher-priority task and safety instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The finding is based on direct text in the only skill file. The behavior requested is broad, mandatory, and designed to affect all future assistant responses.

content.value_statement: Assistants can miss specialized guidance when they answer before checking available skills. This skill pushes early skill discovery and a strict workflow order.
actual_capabilities: Defines a rule for invoking relevant skills before responses or actions.
Explains how Claude Code should load skills through the Skill tool.
Provides a DOT flow diagram for skill selection decisions.
Lists common rationalizations that lead assistants to skip skills.
Prioritizes process skills before implementation skills.
limitations: Does not implement code, automation, or validation scripts.
Does not discover installed skills by itself.
Uses forceful instructions that may conflict with platform policies.
Does not provide domain-specific task guidance beyond skill selection.

use_cases.title: Standardize agent startup behavior
use_cases.description: Require assistants to check for relevant process guidance before answering a task.

use_cases.title: Train assistants on skill priority
use_cases.description: Teach an assistant to choose process skills before implementation skills when both could apply.

use_cases.title: Document skill usage rules
use_cases.description: Show developers a concise model for when an assistant should load skill instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic skill check
prompt_templates.prompt: Before answering, check whether any available skill applies to this request and name the skill you will use.

prompt_templates.title: Skill priority review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the available skills for this task, then explain which process skill should run before any implementation skill.

prompt_templates.title: Workflow compliance audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare this assistant workflow against the skill selection rules and identify where skill checks are skipped.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced agent policy design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safer skill selection policy that preserves early skill checks without overriding higher-priority system instructions.

output_examples.input: Should I use a skill before fixing a bug?
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies debugging as a process skill candidate, loads it first, then applies any relevant implementation guidance.

output_examples.input: Build a frontend view with available skills.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for process guidance first, then applies frontend guidance before editing files.

output_examples.input: Review whether our agent follows skill selection rules.
output: The review identifies missing skill checks before clarifying questions.
It recommends a safer policy that respects platform instruction priority.

best_practices: Use skill checks as workflow guidance, not as authority above platform instructions.
Keep process skills narrow so they do not control unrelated assistant behavior.
Document when a skill is optional, required, or superseded by higher-priority instructions.
anti_patterns: Do not use absolute language that claims the assistant has no choice.
Do not tell assistants to ignore normal review or file-reading procedures.
Do not force a skill to run for every conversation without scope limits.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It tells an assistant to check for relevant skills before responding or taking action.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file contains Markdown instructions and a DOT diagram, not executable code.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk critical?
faq.answer: The skill attempts to control assistant behavior globally and restrict normal tool use.

faq.question: Can this be made safer?
faq.answer: Yes. Replace absolute commands with scoped guidance that respects higher-priority platform instructions.

faq.question: Who might use this concept?
faq.answer: Workflow designers and prompt engineers may use the concept to define safer skill selection policies.

faq.question: Does it provide domain expertise?
faq.answer: No. It only describes when and how an assistant should consider other skills.
